title: The New Normal: Why Millions of Young Americans Are Staying Home
subtitle: High housing costs and inflation are changing the path to adulthood for a generation of workers.

summary.en: A record number of young adults in the United States are living with their parents due to rising housing costs. New data shows that over 25 million people between the ages of 25 and 35 remain in their childhood homes. Experts note that this trend is driven by expensive rent and home prices rather than a lack of employment. Many young professionals are struggling to save enough money to move out or buy their first home. This shift is also affecting parents, who may now need to delay their own retirement plans.
summary.zh: 由於住房成本不斷攀升，美國與父母同住的年輕成年人數量創下歷史新高。最新數據顯示，超過 2500 萬名 25 歲至 35 歲的民眾仍居住在兒時家中。專家指出，此趨勢是由高昂的租金與房價所驅動，而非缺乏就業機會。許多青年專業人士難以存到足夠的錢搬出去住或購買第一間房。這種轉變也影響了父母，他們可能因此需要延後自己的退休計畫。

paragraphs.en: In a trend that continues to reshape American life, a record number of young adults are living with their parents. According to new data from Realtor.com, 25.2 million people between the ages of 25 and 35 were living at home in 2025. This represents one-third of all young adults in that age group, a significant increase from the patterns seen in the early 2000s.
paragraphs.zh: 一項持續重塑美國生活的趨勢顯示，與父母同住的年輕成年人數量創下歷史新高。根據房地產網站 Realtor.com 的最新數據，2025 年有 2520 萬名年齡介於 25 歲至 35 歲之間的民眾住在家中。這佔了該年齡層所有年輕成年人的三分之一，與 2000 年代初期的情況相比，增幅相當顯著。

paragraphs.en: While previous discussions often suggested that young people might be living at home because they could not find work, the current situation tells a different story. The data from Realtor.com highlights that 70% of these young adults have jobs. Many even hold college degrees. Hannah Jones, a senior economist at the company, explained that this is not a story about young people failing to find employment. Instead, it is a story about the high cost of housing making independent living impossible for many, even those with steady incomes.
paragraphs.zh: 過去的討論常認為年輕人住在家中是因為找不到工作，但目前的情況卻截然不同。Realtor.com 的數據強調，這些年輕成年人中有 70% 擁有工作，許多人甚至具備大學學歷。該公司的資深經濟學家瓊斯（Hannah Jones）解釋，這並非年輕人無法就業的問題，而是高昂的居住成本讓許多人即便擁有穩定收入，也無法實現獨立生活。

paragraphs.en: The financial pressure on this generation is clear. The national median rent is now 18% higher than it was before the pandemic, while the typical price to buy a home has jumped by 34%. These costs have created a difficult environment for anyone trying to start their own household. As Jones noted, every young adult who stays in a childhood bedroom represents a lease that was never signed and a starter home that remains unpurchased. This lack of movement in the housing market makes it even harder for others to find affordable places to live, as there is less turnover in available properties.
paragraphs.zh: 這一代人面臨的財務壓力顯而易見。全國租金中位數目前比疫情前高出 18%，而購買房屋的典型價格則飆升了 34%。這些成本為任何試圖建立自己家庭的人創造了艱難的環境。正如瓊斯所指出的，每一位留在兒時臥室的年輕成年人，都代表著一份未簽署的租約，以及一間未被購買的入門級住宅。房地產市場缺乏流動性，使得其他人更難找到負擔得起的居住空間，因為可供選擇的房產周轉率降低了。

paragraphs.en: Economic challenges beyond housing are also playing a role. Recent reports from the Bureau of Labor Statistics show that inflation — the general increase in prices for goods and services — recently reached a three-year high. This rise in costs has effectively removed the benefits of wage increases that many workers received over the past year. Furthermore, the job market for recent graduates remains tough. Roughly 40% of those who finished college recently are considered underemployed, meaning they are working in roles that do not require the degrees they worked so hard to earn.
paragraphs.zh: 除了住房之外，其他經濟挑戰也發揮了作用。美國勞工統計局（Bureau of Labor Statistics）的近期報告顯示，通貨膨脹（即商品與服務價格的普遍上漲）近期達到三年來的新高。物價上漲實際上抵銷了許多勞工過去一年獲得的加薪效益。此外，應屆畢業生的就業市場依然嚴峻。近期大學畢業生中，約有 40% 被視為「就業不足」，意即他們所從事的工作並不需要他們辛苦取得的學位。

paragraphs.en: This situation has long-term consequences for both the young adults and their parents. For the young, delaying the move to their own home means they are missing out on building wealth through property ownership. The age of the typical first-time home buyer has now climbed to 40. For the parents, often called "not-so-empty nesters," the presence of adult children can change their own financial future. Many parents are finding that they must delay their retirement, put off plans to move to smaller homes, or reduce the amount of money they are able to save for their later years.
paragraphs.zh: 這種情況對年輕成年人及其父母都產生了長期影響。對年輕人而言，延後搬出家門意味著他們錯失了透過持有房產來累積財富的機會。目前首次購屋者的平均年齡已攀升至 40 歲。對於常被稱為「非空巢族」的父母來說，成年子女在家中居住可能會改變他們自己的財務未來。許多父母發現，他們必須延後退休、推遲搬往較小住宅的計畫，或是減少為晚年生活所能儲蓄的金額。

paragraphs.en: Looking ahead, experts suggest that this trend is unlikely to change quickly. When comparing current figures to the early 2000s, the shift is striking. If the living patterns from two decades ago had continued, there would be nearly 5 million fewer young adults living with their parents today. The current reality is that for many young professionals, staying at home is the only viable financial option available to them. It is not that they lack the desire for independence, but rather that they lack the means to achieve it in an economy where the cost of living has outpaced their earnings. As inflation continues to impact daily life, the prospect of moving out remains a distant goal for millions of Americans.
paragraphs.zh: 展望未來，專家認為這種趨勢短期內不太可能改變。將當前數據與 2000 年代初期相比，轉變相當驚人。如果二十年前的生活模式持續至今，現在與父母同住的年輕成年人將會減少近 500 萬人。現實情況是，對許多青年專業人士而言，住在家中是他們唯一可行的財務選擇。並非他們缺乏獨立的渴望，而是因為在生活成本成長速度超越薪資的經濟環境下，他們缺乏實現獨立的經濟能力。隨著通貨膨脹持續影響日常生活，搬出去住對數百萬美國人來說，依然是一個遙遠的目標。

keyTerms.definitionEn: To possess or have a specific qualification or job.
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作「拿著、握住」，這裡指擁有學位。文中：Many even hold college degrees.

keyTerms.definitionEn: The rate at which people or things are replaced within a system.
keyTerms.definitionZh: （人員或物品的）流動率、更替率。
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見作「營業額」，這裡指房屋市場的流動性。文中：This lack of movement in the housing market makes it even harder for others to find affordable places to live, as there is less turnover in available properties.

keyTerms.definitionEn: To grow or develop faster than something else.
keyTerms.noteZh: 由 out- 和 pace 組成，指速度領先。文中：It is not that they lack the desire for independence, but rather that they lack the means to achieve it in an economy where the cost of living has outpaced their earnings.

sources.name: The Guardian Business
sources.title: Not so empty nesters: record-high number of US adults under 35 live at home, new data says

previousArticles.title: Why More Young Americans Are Living With Their Parents
previousArticles.summaryEn: A record number of young adults in the United States are currently living with their parents. New data shows that over 25 million people between the ages of 25 and 35 are staying in their childhood homes. Experts say this is not because these young people lack jobs, but because housing has become too expensive. This trend is affecting both the young adults and their parents' retirement plans. The lack of new buyers in the housing market is also making it harder for others to find affordable homes.

quiz.question: According to the article, what is the primary reason for the increase in the number of young adults living at home compared to the early 2000s?

options.A: A lack of job opportunities for college graduates.
options.B: The high cost of housing relative to income.
options.C: A decrease in the total number of available rental properties.
options.D: A cultural shift preferring multi-generational living.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 B。根據文章第二段，經濟學家 Hannah Jones 明確指出，這並非年輕人找不到工作（70% 都有工作），而是房價與租金過高，使得獨立生活變得不可能。選項 A 錯誤，因為文中提到多數年輕人是有工作的。選項 C 雖提到租屋市場缺乏流動性，但這是結果而非原因。選項 D 文中並未提及文化偏好，而是強調財務上的無奈。

quiz.question: What can be inferred about the impact of adult children living with their parents on the parents' own life plans?

options.A: Parents are able to save more money due to shared household expenses.
options.B: Parents are successfully downsizing their homes earlier than expected.
options.C: Parents are facing financial setbacks that may force them to alter their retirement timelines.
options.D: Parents are experiencing a decrease in their overall cost of living.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。根據文章第五段，這些被稱為「非空巢族」（not-so-empty nesters）的父母，因為成年子女在家，被迫延後退休、延後搬至較小房屋的計畫，並減少退休儲蓄。選項 A 與 D 與文中描述的「財務壓力」相反。選項 B 錯誤，因為文中明確指出父母被迫「延後」搬遷計畫，而非提早進行。

quiz.question: In the phrase 'not-so-empty nesters,' what does the term 'nesters' refer to in the context of the article?

options.A: Parents whose children have moved out of the house.
options.B: Young adults who are still living in their childhood bedrooms.
options.C: People who are actively looking for new homes to buy.
options.D: Economists who study housing market trends.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 A。「Empty nest」（空巢）在英語中是一個慣用語，指孩子長大離家後，父母獨自居住的狀態。因此「nesters」指的就是這些父母。文章使用「not-so-empty」來反諷，表示雖然孩子應該要離家，但實際上還住在家裡。選項 B 描述的是子女而非父母。選項 C 與 D 與此慣用語的定義無關。

quiz.question: What is the central message of this article?

options.A: Young adults today are less motivated to achieve independence than previous generations.
options.B: The housing market is currently experiencing a record-breaking surplus of starter homes.
options.C: Inflation is the only factor preventing young adults from moving out of their parents' homes.
options.D: Economic barriers, particularly housing costs, are forcing a record number of young adults to remain in their parents' homes.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 D。全文的核心在於探討年輕人與父母同住的現象，並歸因於高房價、通貨膨脹及薪資成長停滯等經濟障礙。選項 A 錯誤，文章強調是「缺乏手段」而非「缺乏意願」。選項 B 與文中提到的「缺乏流動性」相反。選項 C 過於絕對，文中也提到了就業市場與薪資問題，不僅僅是通貨膨脹。
